title: Actionable Privacy for Smart Environments
author: Jannis Strecker-Bischoff
description:
Increasingly, we are surrounded by smart devices that process (personal) data about us. We see a growing trend of such smart environments not only in industrial settings but also in educational spaces and at home. In our private spaces, we typically consent to the data processing of such devices when we set them up (e.g., a smart speaker, or a smart TV). However, other devices may process our personal data without explicit consent (e.g., in public spaces, rental homes, or friends’ houses). While these data can be used in favor of the user, for example to provide personalized experiences (e.g., making a space comfortable after a long day of work), these data can also be used for ad targeting, or it can be sold to third parties. To make users aware of potential privacy risks, researchers have proposed different types of privacy labels (e.g., printed on the box of smart devices). Although these privacy labels are useful for creating awareness, they do not help users to take action for protecting their privacy. Based on early efforts to create Mixed Reality applications to display such privacy labels in the context of use, this project aims at creating automatic means for detecting privacy risks for different type of users of smart environments (e.g., based on privacy personas). Once these risks are identified, possible options that can help people reduce those risks should be offered (e.g., the option to disable a microphone, blur a camera stream, or enact the user's legal right to be forgotten).

--- Context ---
In a previous project, we explored how different users might prefer different Mixed Reality user interfaces. Hence, a system exists that you will build upon, and a lot of literature has already been explored. For this project, we propose that you make use of visual object detection to find and categorize IoT devices in the environment, analyze their potentially privacy-threatening functionality, and provide users with options to act upon their rights (e.g., data deletion or transfer requests) through a Mixed Reality interface (by enriching the existing one).
_raw_planned_method:
- Explore means to identify IoT device capabilities and connect them to privacy risks (e.g., using an ontology, using an LLM, etc.)
- If using W3C WoT Thing Descriptions, you can expand them with privacy-related information
- Work towards generalizing/improving the current Mixed Reality application (it is currently designed only for a few devices)
- Use object detection to identify objects in a room and access their Thing Descriptions
- Investigate how to deal with bystander privacy in this context
- Connect other legal rights (e.g., "right to be forgotten") to the prototype

title: AI as Source Code Substitute: Predictive Execution for Software Methods
author: Guido Salvaneschi
description:
Can we remove the source code and still pass the tests?
This project explores LLM-based predictive execution: given a function signature, docstring, and concrete inputs, an LLM returns the output directly, no source code generation or program execution. You will (i) design prompts/agents that act as “virtual runtimes,” (ii) plug them into a collected benchmark, and (iii) evaluate reliability, latency, and cost against traditional code-generation baselines. The goal is to understand when LLMs can replace code with reasoning, and how to verify such results at scale.
requirements: Programming in a high-level language, basics of software engineering
_raw_planned_method:
Coding (Testing & Oracles)
Programming Languages (Specifications & Semantics)
AI for Code (Prompting, Tool Use, Self-Consistency)

Objective 1 — Dataset & Harness: Find a Python benchmark that feeds tasks (signature + docstring + inputs) to an LLM “oracle” and runs unit tests on the returned outputs.
Objective 2 — Prompt/Agent Design: Develop and compare prompting strategies (e.g., chain-of-thought, self-consistency, few-shots) to improve correctness.
Objective 3 — Baselines & Metrics: Establish strong baselines (code generation + execution) and report accuracy (pass rates), robustness (edge cases), runtime, and costs.
Objective 4 — Data Analysis: Characterize task types where predictive execution excels/fails (numeric precision, recursion/loops, data structure manipulations) and propose mitigation strategies.
Objective 5 — Reproducibility: Release a framework to reproduce results across models and datasets.
_raw_contact_information: Prof. Guido Salvaneschi - Dr. Luca di Grazia

title: AI Evaluation and Multi-Perspective Reasoning
author: Christina Niklaus
description:
Current AI systems are typically evaluated against a single ground truth. But in political, ethical, and societal domains, persistent disagreement is not noise. Instead, it reflects competing rational frameworks.

We offer Bachelor's and Master's thesis projects exploring new computational approaches to:
* AI evaluation beyond ground truth
* Structured multi-perspective reasoning
* Computational models of rational disagreement
* Epistemic responsibility in AI systems

Topics may involve structured LLM generation, formal reasoning models, evaluation framework design, or experimental validation.

Projects are part of an ongoing research project at the intersection of machine learning, formal modeling, and AI ethics.

For more details, reach out to christina.niklaus@unisg.ch or thomas.huber@unisg.ch

title: AI-Driven Forecasting and Decision Support for Active Sourcing in Electronic Component Circular Markets
author: Bernhard Bermeitinger
description:
The aim of this research proposal is to define the research framework for developing AI-driven forecasting models and decision-support systems for GreenChips. The objective is to enable GreenChips to shift from a reactive, push-based inventory model toward an active sourcing strategy based on predictive insights into market availability and price trends of electronic components.

The research questions are designed to be addressed either individually within the scope of a Bachelor’s or Master’s thesis. Each question represents an independent yet complementary contribution toward the overall goal of building an AI-supported active sourcing framework for GreenChips.

GreenChips operates a circular business model, helping OEMs and EMSs monetize excess stock of electronic components while reducing e-waste. Currently, sourcing is reactive: only parts actively uploaded to the marketplace are sold. The global availability of electronic components strongly influences prices and liquidity. Predicting future availability and pricing would enable GreenChips to shift towards active, data-driven sourcing, increasing profitability and resilience. 
Despite having extensive historical availability and pricing data (2018–2025), challenges persist: Sparse, noisy, and irregular time series data (gaps, duplicated inventory, false signals; lack of clear causal understanding between availability and price; and limited visibility into production cycles or upstream supply chain disruptions.


The thesis should focus on these objectives:
- Build predictive models for component availability and prices despite data imperfections.
- Understand the causal relationship between availability, price, and supply chain dynamics.
- Detect latent production cycles and supply chain events from market data.
- Design an AI-driven sourcing decision system based on predictions.
- Evaluate risks, failure modes, and ethical concerns of deploying AI in this market.

There are 6 research questions regarding this topic. The student should choose accordingly and in close collaboration with the supervisor and the responsible contact from GreenChips

- RQ1: How can we model and predict the future availability of electronic components using sparse, noisy, and irregular time series data?
- RQ2: What is the causal relationship between component availability and price evolution?
- RQ3: Can we detect latent production cycles, supply shocks, or demand spikes purely from aggregated availability and price time series?
- RQ4: How can predictive models be operationalized into an AI-driven active sourcing and pricing strategy?
- RQ5: What are the risks, failure modes, and ethical considerations when deploying predictive models for market availability and pricing?
requirements: There are no requirements. Knowledge in data handling, data science, and ML will be beneficial.
_raw_planned_method:
The methodology depends on the research question. However, they all start with a large amount of data engineering: developing ingestion pipelines, cleaning pipelines, fluctuation detection, gap handling, data enrichment, and other metadata like economic indicators or product lifecycle flags.

- Compare different Time-Series Models (TFT, LSTM, DeepAR, N-BEATS)
- Granger causality tests, counterfactual modeling
- Fourier/Spectral analysis for cycle detection, wavelet transforms for multi-scale trend shifts
- Robustness tests: simulate data drift
- Explainable AI-techniques: SHAP, attention visualization

title: Analyzing Agentic (Machine-to-Machine) Payments on Blockchains
author: Ingrid Bauer-Hänsel
description:
ayments are increasingly initiated not only by humans but also by software systems that transact autonomously 24/7 – e.g., services calling other services, APIs monetizing access per request, or machine-operated workflows requiring programmable settlement. In parallel, public and permissioned blockchains have developed into internet-based settlement layers where digital value (especially stablecoins) can be transferred almost instantly. This has created a credible design space for machine-to-machine / agentic payments on-chain and outside of the traditional banking sector.
Prominent recent developments are x402, an “HTTP-native” payment protocol that enables programmatic payments directly within standard web request/response flow, and Cloudflare announcing plans to launch NET Dollar, which aims to provide infrastructure for the “AI-driven” internet. At the same time, “agentic payment” use cases are implemented without a single shared standard – for example through direct stablecoin transfers, bespoke billing/checkout flows, or wallet-layer primitives (e.g. account abstraction, paymasters).
While there is growing academic work on related mechanisms such as intent-based execution and solver markets, this literature largely focuses on DeFi trade execution, market design, and risks, rather than on general-purpose payment protocols for APIs/services or on comparisons to traditional payment rails (credit card payments, wire transfers, SWIFT).
requirements:
Your Profile
•	A strong interest in financial technologies, digital currencies, or blockchain infrastructure.
•	Ideally, prior exposure to cryptocurrency markets or blockchain analytics tools.
•	Strong analytical and quantitative reasoning skills.
•	Familiarity with data collection and visualization tools (e.g., Python, R, Power BI) is beneficial.
•	Ability to work independently and synthesize complex information.
•	Proficiency in English (written and spoken); German skills are a plus but not mandatory.

We Offer
•	The opportunity to contribute to a timely and high-impact research area with real-world relevance.
•	The chance to engage with one of the most dynamic segments of the digital finance space.
•	In-depth academic supervision, including guidance on quantitative methods and literature integration.
•	The possibility to collaborate with blockchain observatories or analytics providers (optional).
•	Flexible start date; the thesis is expected to be completed within ±6 months.
•	Potential for your findings to be published or presented in academic or industry settings.

Application
If you are ready to take on this challenging and rewarding project, please apply with:
•	Your CV.
•	Academic transcripts.
•	A proposed timeline for the thesis.
•	A motivation letter (max. 500 words), outlining your interest in blockchain technologies and empirical research
_raw_planned_method: This thesis analyzes agentic (machine to machine) payments on blockchains. Building on a structured review of academic and practitioner literature, it develops an evaluation framework and applies it to selected on chain payment approaches and protocol design, including x402, while positioning traditional payment rails only as a high level reference. The analysis examines existing payment flows on blockchains, protocol designs, and identifies key factors for practical adoption (e.g. incentives, performance, implementation, identity/permissioning, reputation, interoperability, settlement systems). To ground the analysis empirically, the thesis may include a limited showcase implementation or simulation of an agentic payment flow to illustrate protocol behavior under realistic assumptions.

title: Assisted Writing for “Vibe Coding” Documentation
author: Edona Elshan
description:
What to investigate How AI writing support (e.g., DeepL Write/Translate) affects the quality, consistency, and maintainability of method documentation for “Vibe Coding.” Examine impacts on writer productivity and reader comprehension, and how AI support shapes tone, terminology, and structure.
Possible research question RQ: How does AI-assisted writing influence documentation quality, comprehension, and maintenance effort for the Vibe Coding method?
Suggested method Mixed methods.
1. Design a documentation task (students or junior researchers produce/translate/improve Vibe Coding docs with/without DeepL support).
2. Quantitative evaluation: rubric-based quality ratings (clarity, coherence, completeness, consistency), time-on-task, revision counts; comprehension tests with independent readers.
3. Qualitative analysis: think-aloud/protocol analysis to capture decision points; interview writers and readers on trust, fluency, and perceived control.
4. Text analytics: style/lexical diversity/terminology consistency.
Expected outcome Evidence on when AI helps vs. harms documentation; a documentation style guide and AI-support checklist for Vibe Coding; a reproducible evaluation rubric; and design implications for integrating AI in method handbooks and teaching materials.
_raw_planned_method: Mixed methods

title: Automated Climate Data Management Using Machine Learning and LLMs
author: Simon Mayer
description:
The University of St. Gallen (HSG) is committed to achieving Net Zero emissions. To track progress, the Sustainability Services Team reports annually on the university’s greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions across multiple categories, including procurement, business travel, commuting, and energy use. These calculations are currently based primarily on spend-based data (e.g., invoices and receipts). 

At present, the data collection, processing, categorisation, and reporting workflow is largely manual. As a result, it is time-consuming, error-prone, and difficult to scale as data volumes and reporting requirements increase. 

# Desired Outcome of this Project

The objective of this project is to design, extend, and evaluate an automated climate data management system that supports GHG accounting through: 
•	Automated extraction and categorisation of invoice-based data
•	Reliable calculation and visualisation of GHG emissions
•	Confidence-aware outputs that identify cases requiring human review
•	Improved scalability, transparency, and usability for sustainability practitioners

Building on an existing prototype that has been developed during a Bachelor Project by Computer Science students (see attached screenshot), the thesis aims to further develop the system toward a robust, production-ready solution that can support institutional climate reporting and decision-making. 

While the project is immediately relevant to HSG, the challenges addressed are highly relevant beyond the university context. Many organisations face similar difficulties when managing climate and sustainability data. A successful solution therefore has strong potential for transferability and application in other institutional or corporate settings. 


# Available Data 

The thesis will build on existing datasets used by the Sustainability Services Team which reflects real-world challenges commonly encountered in industry, such as heterogeneous formats, varying data quality, scanned documents, images, and handwritten annotations. Data include:
•	Historical invoice and receipt data related to various emission categories, such as travel and procurement
•	Spend-based data for all emission categories dating back to 2019
•	Previously categorised data from a pilot project on travel emissions conducted with bachelor students (September 2025 – January 2026)
_raw_planned_method:
The project is expected to make use of generative AI, modern machine learning, and other data processing techniques, including:
•	Large Language Models (LLMs) for text and document understanding
•	Image-based analysis (e.g., OCR, multimodal LLMs) for scanned invoices and receipts
•	Machine-learning methods for automated categorisation and classification (e.g., training models on historical data and prior categorisations)
•	Confidence scoring or uncertainty estimation to support supervised (human-in-the-loop) validation
•	Dashboard and visualisation tools for interactive exploration and use of emissions data
•	Features supporting collaboration, such as assigning emission categories to team members with automated deadline reminders.

Students are encouraged to explore and compare different models and tools, and to propose innovative solutions to data extraction and categorisation challenges.

title: Bewusstseinsverändernde Substanzen
author: Florian Elliker
description:
Bewusstseinsverändernde Substanzen wie Kaffee, Alkohol, Tabak, Cannabis oder Psychedelika sind integraler Teil der Menschheitsgeschichte und stellen auch in unseren Gesellschaften zentrale Aspekte menschlicher Lebenswelten dar. Die anstehenden Debatten zur neuen (gesetzlichen) Regelung solcher Substanzen wirft wichtige Fragen zum gesellschaftlichen Umgang mit ihnen auf, u.a. was die passenden Substanzökonomien sind, welche kulturellen Praktiken zielführend im Umgang mit ihnen sind, wofür und wie solche Substanzen gelingend eingesetzt werden können usw. usf.

Wir sind auf der Suche nach Studierenden, die sich für den gesellschaftlichen Umgang mit Substanzen wie Cannabis oder Psychedelika interessieren (LSD, DMT, Psilocybin, Meskalin, aber auch MDMA, Ketamin etc.). Bei Unexplored Realities (am Seminar für Soziologie an der SHSS) forschen wir gegenwärtig zum alltäglichen Gebrauch von Cannabis und planen in diesem Zusammenhang zwei Cannabis-Pilotversuche. Weiter sind Projekte zur Verwendung von Psychedelika in gegenwärtigen Alltagswelten geplant.

Die Bachelor- oder Masterarbeiten sollen nicht «für die Schublade» geschrieben werden, sondern zum Stand der Forschung in diesem Gebiet beitragen – so, dass dies von Fachkolleg*innen wahrgenommen werden und diskutiert werden kann.

Die theoretischen und/oder empirischen Arbeiten sollen aus einer soziologischen und phänomenologischen Perspektive vor allem zu den Aspekten (Mind-)Set und Setting sowie den Erfahrungsqualitäten geschrieben werden. Denkbar sind auch Diskursanalysen zur Thematik.

Die Forschungsagenda «Zukunft Cannabis» (Open Access) wirft Fragen auf, die in ähnlicher Form auch für Psychedelika relevant sind:
https://www.seismoverlag.ch/de/daten/zukunft-cannabis

Forschungsprojekt «Cannabis im Alltag»:
https://sfs.unisg.ch/de/forschung/forschungsverbund-unexplored-realities/forschungsschwerpunkte/#c46533
(Ggf. muss der Text mit Klick auf den Titel ausgeklappt werden.)
requirements: Falls eine empirische Arbeit geschrieben wird, ist Erfahrung mit qualitativen Methoden von Vorteil. Empirische Arbeiten sind vor allem (aber nicht ausschliesslich) für die Master-Stufe vorgesehen.
_raw_planned_method: Es sind rein theoretische oder aber empirische Arbeiten möglich. Bei empirischen Arbeiten wird primär mit qualitative Forschungsmethoden (nicht standardisierte Interviews und/oder ethnographische Methoden) gearbeitet. Möglich sind aber auch Mixed-Methods-Forschungsdesigns.

title: Bildende Kunst und Gesundheit
author: Harald Tuckermann
description:
Spitäler und andere Organisationen des Gesundheitswesens engagieren sich neben ihrer Kerntätigkeit der Behandlung kranker Menschen auch als Ort für Kunst. Sie bieten Künstler:Innen beispielsweise Flächen zur Darstellung ihrer Werke oder organisieren eigene Ausstellungen in ihren Räumlichkeiten. So führt das Kantonsspital Graubünden (KSGR) beispielsweise eine Ausstellung «Kunst und Bau» (https://www.ksgr.ch/kunst) im Zuge des Arealumbaus durch. Mittels bildender Kunst (Ausstellungen, Begleitanlässe) vernetzt die KK des KSGR Kunst mit medizinischen und allgemeinen hospitalen Bereichen (Garten, Küche, Pflege, etc.). Art & Health, heilende Kunst, soll Patient:innen, Mitarbeitende und Besuchende berühren.

Daneben findet sich zunehmend Forschung über die Wirkung von Kunst auf die Gesundheit, das Wohlergehen oder auf den Heilungsprozess, wie z.B. in der Metastudie der WHO (https://www.who.int/europe/de/news/item/15-11-2023-the-power-of-healing--new-who-report-shows-how-arts-can-help-beat-noncommunicable-diseases). In der Schweiz gibt es demgegenüber kaum Untersuchungen zu dem Thema und auch kaum Fokussierung auf bildende Kunst, die den grössten Teil von Kunstaktivitäten Schweizer Spitäler darstellt. Zur Schliessung dieser Forschungslücke stellt sich daher folgende Frage: Wie lässt sich die Wirkung von Kunst auf Gesundheit, Gesundung oder Wohlbefinden von Menschen in einem Spital aufzeigen?
requirements: Interesse am Thema und idealerweise Vorerfahrung in der Erhebung qualitativer und quantitativer Daten
_raw_planned_method: Die Bearbeitung beinhaltet zwei Teile: Erstens braucht es eine Analyse einschlägiger, internationaler und nationaler Forschungsliteratur zur Identifikation möglicher Wirkungszusammenhänge zwischen Kunst und Gesundheit, Gesundung oder Wohlbefinden. Zweitens sollen diese Wirkungszusammenhänge im Rahmen einer Einzelfallstudie mit dem KSGR validiert werden, z.B. über die Erfassung und Erklärung von Patientenbezogenen Outcomes, wie Verweildauer, Medikation oder Rückfallquoten, Wohlbefinden von Patient:Innen während des Aufenthaltes. Darüber hinaus können – je nach entwickeltem Wirkungsmodell – auch Faktoren erhoben und berücksichtig werden, wie Mitarbeiterzufriedenheit, Arbeitsklima, Stressempfinden. Herausfordernd wird hierbei die Isolation von Wirkungszusammenhängen sein.

title: Collaborative Memory for Personalized Multi-User LLM Interaction Using Solid Pods
author: Jannis Strecker-Bischoff
description:
Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used as personal assistants, knowledge tools, and creative collaborators. Traditionally, LLMs maintain private memory for individual users, storing information across sessions to improve continuity and personalization. However, emerging research suggests the potential for shared memory across multiple users, enabling collaborative interactions where knowledge, context, and insights can be jointly built, referenced, and extended. 
Additionally, research suggests, that personalization becomes increasingly ubiquitous, e.g. through developments in LLMs, XR and UbiComp´. And through this increased pervasiveness, personalized assistants may rather lead to “fragmented realities” where individuals perceive (hyper-) personalized realities [5]. To tackle this, we previously suggested that the sharing of personalized content a across users could be a way to tackle this [4]. As LLMs empower also more and more ubiquitous interfaces (e.g., smart glasses), enabling these to responsible access data from multiple users is an interesting challenge.

Recent works such as “Collaborative Memory: Multi-User Memory Sharing in LLM Agents with Dynamic Access Control” [1] propose architectures for multi-user memory with fine-grained access control, allowing LLMs to store and selectively share knowledge among different participants. Similarly, “Beyond Individual Concerns: Multi-User Privacy in Large Language Models” [2] investigates the privacy implications of multi-user LLM interactions, emphasizing the need to balance knowledge sharing with privacy guarantees. While platforms such as mem0.ai provide mechanisms for persistent memory in LLMs, the design of shared memory systems that are intuitive, and socially meaningful remains an open challenge.

Solid (Social Linked Data) is a specification to create a decentralized data platform for Web applications; its objective is to allow the management of users’ data independently of the applications that create and consume such data: Applications are decoupled from the data they use. To realize Solid, users' data is stored in a Web-accessible personal online datastore: a Pod. Developers can use the Solid protocol based on existing W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) recommendations for reading, writing, and controlling (e.g., access control) the contents of users’ Pods. Users are able to grant access to their data, and have the option to stop sharing their data or switch between applications at any time [3].

This thesis proposes the design and implementation of a prototype system for shared memory across multiple users’ LLM sessions, where memory graphs are stored in Solid Pods. Using Solid enables each user to maintain control over their memory data while selectively sharing parts of it with e.g., friends, family, or collaborators. The system will leverage mem0.ai as the underlying LLM memory platform and integrate dynamic access controls inspired by [1]. Users will be able to assign selective read/write permissions, and control visibility for different collaborators. 

The thesis will further explore the integration of shared LLM memory, Solid Pods, and social interaction. Concretely, it will implement a collaborative interface that provides:
- A suitable UI for managing memory graphs stored in Solid Pods, including adding, removing, and tagging memory items.
- Selective sharing controls, enabling users to decide what memory is private, shared with specific users, or public to a group, using the access control mechanisms of Solid.
- Support for real-time referencing of shared memory across LLM sessions, facilitated through decentralized storage.

This project is great for you, if you are:
- Interested in Personalization, LLMs, Privacy, & Decentralized data storage
- Interested in participating in state of the art research
- Interested in publishing your research work in academic venues
- An excellent communicator in English

References
[1] M. Fu, L. Li, J. Zhang, Collaborative Memory: Multi-User Memory Sharing in LLM Agents with Dynamic Access Control, arXiv:2505.18279 (under review at ICLR 2026).
[2] L. Li, E. Bertino, Beyond Individual Concerns: Multi-User Privacy in Large Language Models, ACM, 2022.
[3] Essam Mansour Sambra, Sandro Hawke, Maged Zereba, Nicola Greco, Abdurrahman Ghanem, D. Zagidulin, Ashraf Aboulnaga, and T. Berners-Lee. Solid: A Platform for Decentralized Social Applications Based on Linked Data, 2016.

title: Context-Aware AI for Anomaly Detection in Industrial Systems
author: Karim Khamaisi
description:
This thesis offers a chance to build the next generation of industrial AI. We're tackling a major hurdle in smart factories: making sense of data from multiple sensors (acoustic, vibration, pressure) that is often messy, sparse, and asynchronous. Crucially, the relevance of each sensor constantly shifts based on the machine’s operational context (its activity level) and the environment (ambient noise) [1]. 

Your goal is to develop an intelligent AI framework that acts like a highly experienced engineer. It will continuously learn the machine's context and dynamically decide which sensor is most reliable at that moment. This means catching machine anomalies earlier and making the entire monitoring system smarter and more energy-efficient [2]. 

Example 1 – High Load vs. Idle Context: When a pump is at high load, the vibration sensor is your best friend for spotting mechanical anomalies. But when the pump is idle, external noise corrupts the vibration data, making the pressure sensor the most trustworthy source for assessing system integrity [3]. Your AI will learn this dynamic. 

Example 2 – Normal vs. Noisy Context: If a sudden event (like a vehicle passing) introduces external acoustic noise, the microphone is instantly compromised. Your AI must instantly reduce the 'trust' score on the acoustic data, relying on the clean vibration and pressure readings to maintain continuous, reliable anomaly detection [4], [5]. 

Research Questions 
1) How can we use deep learning to infer the machine’s operational context (e.g., high-load, noisy environment) directly from irregular sensor data? 

2) Can we create an adaptive AI mechanism that uses this context to continuously and dynamically adjust the importance (weight) of each sensor? 

References
[1] Yasaei, R., Hernandez, F., & Faruque, M. A. A. (2020, November). IoT-CAD: Context-aware adaptive anomaly detection in IoT systems through sensor association. In Proceedings of the 39th international conference on computer-aided design (pp. 1-9).

[2] Chemashkin, F. Y., & Zhilenkov, A. A. (2019, January). Fault tolerance control in Cyber-physical systems. In 2019 IEEE Conference of Russian Young Researchers in Electrical and Electronic Engineering (EIConRus) (pp. 1169-1171). IEEE.

[3] Zhao, Y., Yang, X., Huang, J., Wu, X., & Cui, J. (2024). Adaptive weighting strategy for fault detection and diagnosis of rotating machinery components incorporating multiple operational conditions. Transactions of the Institute of Measurement and Control, 46(5), 832-844.

[4] Qu, X., Liu, Z., Wu, C. Q., Hou, A., Yin, X., & Chen, Z. (2024). Mfgan: multimodal fusion for industrial anomaly detection using attention-based autoencoder and generative adversarial network. Sensors, 24(2), 637.

[5] Kullu, O., & Cinar, E. (2022). A deep-learning-based multi-modal sensor fusion approach for detection of equipment faults. Machines, 10(11), 1105.
requirements: Python skills are essential, particularly with data libraries like NumPy and Pandas. Familiarity with deep learning frameworks (PyTorch/TensorFlow) is a major plus. You'll likely work with synthetic data scenarios representing real-world machine contexts like high-load or noisy states.
_raw_planned_method: This thesis brings together system design and data analysis. You'll begin by creating realistic scenarios using simulated industrial data from sources like acoustic sensors, vibration measurements, and pressure readings. The core of your work involves building two key components: a Latent Context Encoder that figures out the current operating conditions, and a Gating Network that adjusts how much weight to give each sensor based on those conditions. You'll use deep learning to train this system to improve anomaly detection. By the end, you'll have a comparison showing how well your adaptive approach works compared to traditional methods that use fixed sensor weights.

title: Course Check and Revision Strategies
author: Jérémy Lemee
description:
We are considering software agents autonomously exploring the Web to achieve a given goal, typically provided by a human user, for example an online shopping goal.
  When such an agent is seeking to achieve a goal, it can encounter obstacles that prevent its current course of action from succeeding. This is where CCRS (Course Check and Revision Strategies), a new concept that we are developing, can be used to help the agent develop a new course of action when its current course of action fails. For example, in online shopping, a required item may become unavailable, thus requiring the agent to either fail the goal or develop an alternative strategy. CCRS may rely on techniques such as planning [1], or reinforcement learning  [2], that are directly implemented by the agent. CCRS may also rely on external services such as recommender systems. These different techniques could be selected at run time by an agent. For example, in a shopping scenario, a recommender system could be used by an agent to find out alternative products, if such a recommender is found in the agent's environment. 
The project of the Master Thesis is to develop a general model of CCRS, and a resulting classification of known CCRS, that different types of agents (from logic-based agents [3] and BDI agents [4] to agents based on Large Language Models [5]) can use. We will consider these agents to act within open and dynamic environments Web-based environments, where we expect CCRS to be most useful.

[1] Ghallab, M., Nau, D., & Traverso, P. (2004). Automated Planning: theory and practice. Elsevier.

[2] Sutton, R. S. (2018). Reinforcement learning: An introduction. A Bradford Book.

[3] Wooldridge, M. (1999). Intelligent agents. Multiagent systems: A modern approach to distributed artificial intelligence, 1, 27-73.

[4] Rao, A. S., & Georgeff, M. P. (1995, June). BDI agents: from theory to practice. In Icmas (Vol. 95, pp. 312-319).

[5] Sumers, T. R., Yao, S., Narasimhan, K., & Griffiths, T. L. (2023). Cognitive architectures for language agents. arXiv preprint arXiv:2309.02427.
requirements:
Interest on Agents/Multi-Agent Systems and basic knowledge on this topic
Basic knowledge on planning and machine learning.
_raw_planned_method:
The thesis will follow the steps: 
1. Study literature on mechanisms that can be interpreted as CCRS. 
2. Build general model for CCRS. 
3. Create classification of CCRS. 
4. Develop a general mechanism for selecting and applying CCRS. 
5. Implement this mechanism in BDI agents and LLM-based agents.

title: Data-Driven Business Model Innovation for Robotics Start-up
author: Lena-Marie Pätzmann
description:
Aiina Robotics develops modular robots for the construction industry, initially focusing on concrete renovation. Similar to the start-up ProGlove, which transitioned from hardware to a software-based business model, we aim to explore the potential of data analytics derived from our robots. The goal of this thesis is to analyze whether and how data collected by robots on construction sites can be monetized—both in the field of concrete renovation and for additional applications.

Objective of the Thesis: Examine whether an expansion of the business model from hardware (leasing and selling robots) to data analytics services is viable. The thesis should explore the potential of collected robot data and identify monetization opportunities.

Research Focus Areas: 
•	Customer Value Analysis:
Analyze the customer value of robot-collected data compared to camera-based data. Investigate whether the data (e.g., material condition, temperature, movement) provides added value to construction companies—not only in concrete renovation but also in other construction tasks. 
•	Economic Viability Analysis:
Assess the economic viability of expanding the business model from hardware to data analytics, similar to ProGlove’s example (e.g., predictive maintenance, workflow optimization). Determine how construction companies could save costs or generate additional revenue through data analytics services. Develop a pricing and monetization model for data services.
•	Technical Feasibility Assessment:
Evaluate the technical feasibility of integrating sensors into robots for continuous real-time data collection and making this data accessible for construction companies. Investigate the necessary infrastructure for data utilization and analysis.

title: Design and Implementation of a Scalable Architecture for Cloud Infrastructure
author: Guido Salvaneschi
description:
Background
The European Open Compute Architecture for Powerful Edge (CAPE) is a Horizon research project funded by the European Union. CAPE aims to revolutionize edge-cloud computing by developing an open, standardized hardware platform integrated with a cloud-agnostic system called Infrastructure from Code (IfC). IfC represents an emerging paradigm that allows developers to automatically derive infrastructure requirements directly from application code, eliminating the need for separate configuration files. This innovative approach streamlines infrastructure provisioning, improves consistency, and minimizes the potential for errors.
The CAPE project addresses critical challenges, such as enhancing Europe’s technological sovereignty, fostering interoperability among diverse systems, and optimizing resource utilization. Its solutions are designed to support a wide range of applications, including energy grid management, advanced deep learning systems, and satellite data analysis.
This Thesis contributes to the CAPE initiative by defining the required architecture and components, developing tools for efficient interaction with CAPE resources, and rigorously testing these tools in simulated environments.

Research Objectives
Define architectural and component specifications for the CAPE project to improve resource use and scalability.
Develop a Terraform or Pulumi provider for Nitric to standardize interactions with CAPE resources.
Create a mock CAPE deployment server (API server) to simulate deployment scheduling and validate the solutions.

Research Questions
What architecture and components are needed to improve CAPE’s infrastructure deployment?
How can a Terraform or Pulumi provider for Nitric be designed to effectively interact with CAPE resources?
What features are necessary for a mock CAPE deployment server to test deployment workflows and integration?

Expected Contributions
A detailed architecture and component framework customized for CAPE.
A working Terraform or Pulumi provider for Nitric that interacts with CAPE’s system.
A mock CAPE deployment server for testing deployment workflows.
Validation results showing the effectiveness of the proposed solutions and providing insights for future improvements.
_raw_planned_method:
Requirement Analysis: Study CAPE’s needs and identify key architectural and component requirements.
Current Infrastructure Analysis: Examine existing systems to understand their strengths and weaknesses, documenting how they align with CAPE’s goals.
Provider Development: Design and build a Terraform or Pulumi provider tailored to Nitric for interacting with CAPE resources.
Mock Server Development: Build a mock deployment server that replicates CAPE’s scheduling processes, allowing for controlled testing.
Validation and Testing: Test the solutions to ensure they work reliably, are scalable, and meet CAPE’s requirements in a simulated environment.

title: Design and implementation of an LLM testbed

description:
LLMs are fascinating tools for the economist, but rigorous research requires a systematic, reproducible approach. This thesis, which would be part of a research project, aims at creating a testbed that allows for automating test cases for LLMs and for comparison between test cases. An overly simple example would be to ask the question "What is the capital of France" 100 times to each model, to count the number of correct results and to create a leaderboard of models with their percentages of correct answers. 
This thesis requires knowledge of LLM frameworks, APIs, local LLMs and a bit of ML Ops. If successful, the framework could be published as open source software.

title: Designing Sustainable Business Models for Adaptive Digital Health Platforms: Balancing Scientific Discovery, Societal Impact, and Ethical Value Exchange
author: Tobias Kowatsch
description:
Overview
We are seeking a motivated Master’s student in business, health economics, innovation management, or digital strategy who is passionate about the intersection of AI, ethics, and mental health innovation. This project offers an opportunity to design a sustainable, ethically grounded business model for an evolving AI-driven mental health ecosystem.

Project Purpose & Vision
Digital health tools often struggle to remain both scientifically credible and economically viable. This project aims to design a multi-sided business model for the Gearshift Fellowship (GF) platform that measures and trains adaptability through serious games. GF generates interpretable adaptability markers that support both individual self-reflection and population-level insights into mental health. Built on mathematical algorithms and clinical theories that evolve with scientific progress, GF requires sustainable mechanisms for ongoing research, validation, and ethical data reuse without commodifying users or overpromising outcomes.

Project Goals
The thesis will explore how to align incentives among users, healthcare systems, research institutions, and policymakers to create a self-sustaining ecosystem that evolves with changing symptom trends (e.g., youth suicide, anxiety, and attention dysregulation). It will also examine how adaptive AI systems, which tailor feedback to users based on behavioral data, can enhance user agency responsibly while avoiding manipulation or therapeutic overreach. Lastly, the student will develop a concrete use case and assess market-entry strategies for scaling adaptive digital-health platforms like GF across clinical, corporate, and educational contexts. Key questions to address include:

1. How to sustain and scale GF as an evolving digital infrastructure without compromising ethics or science?
2. How can adaptive AI support agency without manipulation?
3. What funding and data-sharing models best sustain continuous discovery while protecting privacy and autonomy?
4. How can adaptability metrics be positioned as a public good that benefits multiple stakeholders while remaining financially viable?
This project will produce a blueprint for ethical and sustainable AI-driven mental health ecosystems, showing how scientific discovery, user empowerment, and societal benefit can coexist within a viable business model.

Key Tasks
The student will:
1.	Map the multi-sided value ecosystem of adaptive digital health platforms (users, researchers, clinicians, insurers, policymakers, employers).
2.	Develop sustainable business and funding models that align with value-based healthcare and open scientific collaboration.
3.	Conceptually integrate ethical and agentic AI safeguards into these models by defining what adaptability markers should and should not infer, and which engagement “hooks” are ethically acceptable.
4.	Evaluate how governance frameworks and incentive structures can maintain transparency, user trust, and scientific rigor as the platform evolves.

Data & Resources
The student will have access to:
•	Conceptual and preliminary data from ongoing GF studies and strategic documentation.
•	Mentoring on digital health economics, ethical AI, and business model innovation.
•	The opportunity to contribute to a live multi-stakeholder design initiative within CDHI’s ecosystem.

Required Skills
•	Background in health economics, innovation management, digital strategy, or health policy
•	Interest in AI ethics, mental health innovation, and sustainable digital ecosystems
•	Experience with business modeling tools (e.g., Business Model Canvas, system dynamics)
•	Analytical mindset and openness to interdisciplinary collaboration

Supervision
1. Dr. Nadja Ging-Jehli, Adaptive Intelligence & Mental Health Mechanisms Core, Center for Digital Health Interventions (CDHI), ETH Zurich, University of St. Gallen, University of Zurich; 
2. Prof. Dr. Tobias Kowatsch, Center for Digital Health Interventions (CDHI), University of St. Gallen, University of Zurich & ETH Zurich

title: Development of a Concept for the Value Chain of Modular Construction Robots
author: Lena-Marie Pätzmann
description:
Aiina Robotics develops intelligent, modular robots that assist the construction industry in overcoming labor shortages and increasing productivity. Our robots are equipped with advanced safety features and enable autonomous operation in complex environments. To successfully integrate these innovative robots and create long-term value, it is crucial to design the entire value chain – from hardware procurement to assembly, delivery, service, and repair.  

Objective of the Thesis: The goal of this Master's thesis is to develop a process-oriented concept for the value chain of Aiina Robotics' modular robots. The entire lifecycle of the robots, from procurement and production to delivery and maintenance, will be analyzed and designed. Special emphasis will be placed on customer needs, efficiency, and costs along the entire process chain, from the first interaction to post-delivery service.

Research Focus Areas:  
•	Development of Procurement and Assembly Processes: How can robot components be efficiently procured and assembled in a modular production process that also takes space restrictions into account?
•	Customer-Oriented Logistics Processes: How can the robots be delivered quickly, reliably, and cost-effectively to customers, and which processes improve the user journey during delivery?
•	Service and Maintenance Processes: How can an effective maintenance network be established that is tailored to customer needs, and how can repairs be efficiently organized?
•	 Repair Processes in a Rental Model: How can repair and return processes within a rental model be organized to maximize customer service and efficiency of the value chain?

title: Development of Circular Business Models for Industrial Electric Motors: A Comprehensive Value Analysis of Take-Back Processes and Ecosystem Interactions
author: Naomi Haefner
description:
Background:
Electric motors, which consume about 40% of the world's electricity and contribute to 13% of
global carbon emissions, are increasingly adopted across various sectors. The market for
industrial motors is projected to grow significantly, posing challenges for end-of-life
management and the need for strategies to handle high-value materials. The EU anticipates a
substantial increase in demand for critical raw materials by 2050, yet recycling rates remain low.
To achieve Net Zero targets, manufacturers must shift towards a circular economy (CE).
Innovative circular business models, including materials, data, monetary unit and CO2 flows,
can help overcome current limitations and enhance value capture.

Aim of the thesis
The primary objective of this thesis is to evaluate the current state of take-back processes for
electric motors and propose future circular business models (CBM) that can enhance
circularity. The research will:
• Analyze existing projects and literature on value capturing processes.
• Conduct market price volatility analysis for materials in electric motors. Cost structure
and impact of market price variation on product price
• Map current ecosystems and stakeholder interactions. Identify current take back
channels and pain points limiting eZective product take-backs.
• Investigate future trends in supply chains for materials needed for eZicient electric
motors and CBMs with the involved flows (data, materials, monetary unit, CO2…)
• Define ideal design of mature circular ecosystems for motors and develop a strategy to
close the loop, incentivize stakeholders, and capture value.
What We O9er

As part of this master thesis collaboration, you will have access to:
• Comprehensive data from Siemens' internal projects, tools and documents as well as a
broad collection of relevant public research literature.
• Collaboration opportunities with industry experts and the internal network.
• Advanced analytical tools for market and ecosystem analysis.
• Supportive coordination for solving challenges.

What We Expect
• Hold a bachelor's degree in business informatics, business administration, (industrial)
engineering and management, or a related field.
• Demonstrate a results-oriented and structured approach to work with a high level of
motivation.
• Possess excellent presentation and language skills in English; basic German skills would
be an advantage but not mandatory.

title: Digitales Notariat im Erbrecht: Auswirkungen des DNG auf Verfügungen von Todes wegen
author: Lukas Müller
description:
Das Bundesgesetz über die Digitalisierung im Notariat (DNG, BBl 2023 1523) markiert einen Paradigmenwechsel: Es schafft die Rechtsgrundlagen für das elektronische Original öffentlicher Urkunden und deren zentrale Speicherung. Während die Digitalisierung im Handelsregisterrecht bereits fortgeschritten ist, wirft die Anwendung auf das Erbrecht komplexe Fragen auf.

Diese Arbeit soll untersuchen, wie sich die Einführung des elektronischen Originals auf die öffentliche Beurkundung von Verfügungen von Todes wegen (Testamente und Erbverträge) auswirkt. Im Zentrum steht die Frage, ob die Mechanismen des DNG den spezifischen Formzwecken des Erbrechts (Schutz vor Übereilung, Sicherstellung der Testierfähigkeit, Beweissicherheit über Jahrzehnte) gerecht werden. Zudem ist zu klären, wie der elektronische Rechtsverkehr die Abwicklung von Erbschaften (z.B. Verkehr mit Banken und Grundbuchämtern mittels digitaler Erbscheine) beschleunigen kann und welche Risiken dabei entstehen. 

Die konkrete Ausgestaltung und Eingrenzung des Themas erfolgt in gemeinsamer Absprache.
requirements:
Interesse bezüglich Erbrecht und Beurkundungsrecht.

Interesse an Fragen der Rechtsinformatik und der Digitalisierung von Behördenprozessen.

Fähigkeit, komplexe gesetzgeberische Neuerungen kritisch auf ihre Praxistauglichkeit hin zu überprüfen.
_raw_planned_method:
Untersuchung der Botschaft (BBl 2023 1523) und des Entwurfs zum DNG unter besonderer Berücksichtigung der erbrechtlichen Implikationen.

Abgleich der neuen digitalen Formvorschriften mit den materiellrechtlichen Anforderungen des ZGB an Erbverträge und letztwillige Verfügungen (Art. 499 ff. ZGB, Art. 512 ZGB).

Darstellung der praktischen Auswirkungen auf den notariellen Workflow – von der Errichtung der Urkunde bis zur Eintragung in das geplante zentrale Urkundenregister.

title: Doppelte Wesentlichkeitsanalyse für Schweizer KMU
author: Alex Gertschen
description:
Die doppelte Wesentlichkeitsanalyse (DWA) ist ein Instrument der Nachhaltigkeitsberichterstattung und eine Anforderung der Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) der EU. Sie verbindet zwei Perspektiven:

1. Finanzielle Wesentlichkeit: Diese Dimension analysiert, wie Nachhaltigkeitsrisiken und -chancen (z. B. Klimawandel, soziale oder regulatorische Entwicklungen) die finanzielle Leistung und Position eines Unternehmens beeinflussen können.
2. Impact-Wesentlichkeit: Diese Dimension analysiert, wie die Geschäftstätigkeit, Produkte und Dienstleistungen eines Unternehmens auf die soziale und ökologische Umwelt einwirken – positiv und negativ.

Die DWA ist aber auch ein Instrument der strategischen Unternehmensführung und -entwicklung. Sie unterstützt die Unternehmensleitung bei der Antizipation von sowie Anpassung an Entwicklungen im relevanten Kontext.

Das Ziel der Bachelor- oder Masterarbeit ist es, in Zusammenarbeit mit einem Branchenverband eine exemplarische DWA zu erstellen, die den KMU aus der entsprechenden Branche eine Hilfestellung bietet, um 
a) die eigenen wesentlichen Themen zu erkennen;
b) einen Dialog mit den relevanten Stakeholdern zu führen;
c) gegenüber berichtspflichtigen Kunden zu argumentieren, warum gewisse Nachhaltigkeitsinformationen nicht geliefert werden müssen.
_raw_planned_method: Mixed-methods-Ansatz (quantitativer Fragebogen und qualitative Interviews)

title: From Likes to Votes: Political Influence in the Age of Social Media
author: Florian Gasser
description:
Young people are increasingly obtaining information about political topics through platforms such as Instagram, TikTok, or YouTube, while traditional media like television or newspapers are losing relevance for this audience. Political influencers play a special role in this context: they convey content in a relatable, personal, and often entertaining way, clearly distinguishing themselves from traditional politicians. Against this backdrop, the question arises as to what extent political influencers actually influence the voting intentions and behavior of young voters.

Theoretically, this phenomenon can potentially be explained through various approaches. Through parasocial interactions, perceived personal relationships emerge between followers and influencers, fostering trust and credibility. At the same time, influencers contribute to agenda-setting and framing functions by making certain issues visible or presenting them within specific interpretive frameworks. From the perspective of Uses and Gratifications Theory, young users deliberately choose influencers as a source of political information because they offer not only knowledge but also entertainment, orientation, and opportunities for identification. Of course, other theoretical frameworks can also be applied in the thesis.

For this research topic, an experimental design would be particularly suitable, allowing different settings to be tested (e.g., campaign poster vs. influencer message vs. non-political content).
_raw_planned_method: For this research topic, an experimental design would be particularly suitable, allowing different settings to be tested (e.g., campaign poster vs. influencer message vs. non-political content).

title: GenAI in Higher Education — Design and Prototyping of a Multi-Agent System for Oral Examinations
author: Maximilian May
description:
Oral examinations are one of the most effective formats for assessing students’ deep understanding and ability to apply knowledge. Unlike other examination types, they allow examiners to adapt questions in real time, guide struggling students, and explore students' reasoning beyond superficial answers. However, oral exams are also time-consuming, resource-intensive, and difficult to scale in modern higher education. 
Recent advances in Generative AI open up new possibilities for personalized, scalable, and interactive assessment tools. In particular, GenAI-powered multi-agent systems can be designed to autonomously conduct oral examinations — enabling both formal assessment and interactive exam preparation for students.
This project explores the design and prototyping of a multi-agent system for oral examinations.

Possible research directions:
1.	Investigation of relevant multi-agent approaches
•	What existing research and industry projects apply multi-agent systems in learning, assessment, or related interactive applications?
•	What architectures, agent roles, and interaction frameworks (e.g. Model Context Protocol) are used?
•	What insights can inform the development of this project? What gaps remain?
2.	System design
•	What is a suitable multi-agent system architecture for conducting oral examinations?
•	How many agents should the system include, and what should be the scope and responsibility of each agent?
•	Which external tools and data sources should be integrated? How should they communicate?
3.	Prototyping and evaluation
•	Implement core components of the system (e.g. Knowledge Agent, Evaluation Agent, etc.).
•	Evaluate quality, usability, and application scenarios.

title: Generative AI and Time Series Models
author: Peter Gruber
description: Very recent research aims at applying the structure of Large Language Models onto the field of Time Series analysis. The idea is to use the analogy that language is a series of words, while time series are a series of numbers. There are numerous challenges in applying this analogy, but first primitive models, so-called Time Series Transfomes, have ben successfully implemented. The topic of the thesis is to evaluate inhowfar these models can be used to model financial markets and/or macro time series. A pure econoetrics thesis without great application can also be envisaged.
requirements: This thesis is aimed at very strong candidates with a background both in econometrics/statistics/data science AND programming (Python). title: HCI: AI-Induced Cognitive Biases: The Impact of Anchoring and Loss Aversion in Human–AI Decision-Making
author: Johannes Schöning
description: This research explores how cognitive biases embedded in artificial intelligence systems influence and potentially amplify human biases in decision-making. It brings together two thesis ideas centred on key behavioural mechanisms: anchoring and loss aversion. The first thesis idea examines how anchoring bias in generative AI outputs shapes and reinforces human anchoring tendencies in judgement. The second thesis idea investigates how biased investment recommendations from loss-averse or non-loss-averse AI systems affect investors’ risk preferences and whether continued interaction with such systems alters these preferences over time. Together, these thesis ideas aim to provide a deeper understanding of how AI-driven biases interact with human cognition and behaviour, with implications for ethical AI design and decision support systems.
requirements: Data analysis will involve statistical techniques such as regression analysis, repeated-measures ANOVA, and potentially computational modelling of decision behaviour. Skills required include proficiency in experimental design, programming for AI interaction simulations, data collection and cleaning, and statistical analysis using software such as R, Python, or SPSS. A strong understanding of cognitive psychology, behavioural economics, and human–AI interaction principles will also be essential.
_raw_planned_method: The planned methods for these thesis ideas combine experimental design, behavioural analysis, and quantitative modelling. Participants will engage with AI-generated recommendations or judgements in controlled tasks, allowing measurement of changes in anchoring effects or risk preferences.

title: HCI: CitizenVision: Co-Creating the Imagined City
author: Johannes Schöning
description:
In previous work, we explored how citizens can use GenAI within participation processes to visualise their ideas for urban development. There, we observed a recurring pattern: some citizens tend to prompt need-oriented descriptions (e.g. “a family-friendly place”), whereas current models perform better with solution-oriented inputs (e.g. “a playground”).

This thesis aims to support citizens in visualising their ideas by translating their needs into tangible design solutions. Depending on the study level, the thesis could include the following outcomes:
Design and implementation of a prototype that engages citizens in a co-creative process e.g. with reflective questions or layers to generate possible solutions based on their needs.
User-Evaluation of the implemented prototype(s).

title: HCI: GenAI Tools that Empower Human Thinking and Learning
author: Johannes Schöning
description:
Since the launch of ChatGPT, there has been extensive debate about the impact of GenAI on human thinking and learning. At the same time, a growing body of research shows that GenAI can influence human cognition in both positive and negative ways – depending on how and for what purposes it is used.
The goal of this project is to focus on specific activities that are being reshaped by AI (or are likely to be transformed by it in the near future) and what this means for human thinking. The chosen activity or domain can be creative, analytical, reflective, or otherwise, and can be selected based on your interests and experience.

Within this thematic scope, there are two types of projects you could do:
(1)	Designing an AI tool via a user-centred design process followed by an evaluation of it through a user study. This project type is for you, if you love to design and build things.
(2)	Evaluating an existing AI tool/model and more effective usage strategies for it. This project type is for you, if you are excited about research methodology, data analysis etc.
Combinations of both project types may also be possible if your skills and experience permit.
requirements:
- You are keen to better understand how AI can best augment human cognition
- You are interested in a specific task/activity where human thinking may be at risk and/or benefit from better GenAI tools/usage strategies (e.g. from project work/professional experience/hobbies)
- Ideal, but not required: You have access to a group of people (e.g. your network, organisations you are part of, societies etc.) who you could ask to take part in your study
- Project 1: You have experience with building functional prototypes (e.g. web or mobile apps using a tech stack you know well) and are familiar with user studies.
- Project 2: You have experience in qualitative and/or quantitative research methods.
_raw_planned_method:
Project Type 1 – Design and Evaluation of an AI Tool:
In this project, the goal is to choose a task/activity that you are passionate about and/or have experience with (e.g. through your hobbies, previous professional experiences etc.) to then (1) understand how the use of GenAI might currently impact human thinking (through a review of existing literature, interviews etc.) for this type of activity (i.e. what is the current “baseline”), (2) design a tool that tries to mitigate the challenges and leverage the opportunities through a user-centred design process, (3) evaluate the tool through a user study, where participants perform the chosen task using the tool.

Project Type 2 – Evaluate an Existing AI Tool/Model and Usage Strategies for It:
Besides building new AI tools, there is also a need to better understand the impact different usage strategies of existing AI tools/models can have on human cognition (e.g. using them as a devil’s advocate, thought partner, Socratic questioner instead of an answering machine as many people do). The structure of Type 2 is similar to Type 1 but instead of building a tool you would instead evaluate different usage strategies and compare them against. Furthermore, the emphasis would be more on the study design and methodology.

title: HCI: Supporting Reflective Practice in Human–Computer Interaction
author: Johannes Schöning
requirements: An iterative design and evaluation process involving HCI practitioners assesses the tool’s usability and practical relevance. The proposed work contributes to the field by bridging the gap between theoretical understanding and practical application of reflection in HCI. It concludes with recommendations for future research and development to further strengthen reflective practice as a cornerstone of responsible technology design.
_raw_planned_method: To achieve these goals, the research first develops a classification framework that organises 15 existing reflective approaches according to criteria derived from context-of-use analysis and decision-making in human-centred design. Based on empirical insights into practitioners’ needs, design criteria for an interactive Reflection Companion are then formulated. This tool aims to make reflective methods more pragmatically accessible and actionable within real-world design projects.

title: Industrialization of Methane Pyrolysis for Hydrogen and Solid Carbon Production
author: Christian Opitz
description:
Methane pyrolysis is currently on the edge from the demonstrator stage (TRL 7–8) toward industrial-scale implementation. Unlike Steam Methane Reforming (SMR), the dominant but CO2 emitting hydrogen production technology, methane pyrolysis is an emerging technology that decomposes methane into hydrogen and solid carbon without direct CO₂ emissions. When renewable methane is used, the process can even achieve negative CO₂ emissions.
This master’s thesis will assess the industrialization potential of methane pyrolysis, focusing on upscaling, product and market development, and the impact of new policy frameworks related to CO₂ reduction and negative emissions. The main target of the thesis is the development of an upscaling cost model, allowing product cost and production cost calculations as well as market development estimations.
Thereby, both fossil natural gas and sustainable methane sources (including their potential for negative CO₂ emissions) shall be considered as feedstock options.
The master thesis shall be conducted at least partly at the methane pyrolysis demonstrator site of VzDI (see www.vzdi.ch) in Zug (at V-ZUG AG campus), where the technology and industrial supervision will be provided by VzDI and Empa. The supervision concerning economic and policy aspects, as well as grading in line with study regulations, will be provided by University St.Gallen.

title: Intelligent Dashboard for Aggregating and Summarizing Global Market Data
author: Bernhard Bermeitinger
description:
The praxis partner GEBRÜDER WEISS is planning to increase their market knowledge internally but also externally.
For this, they already connect various data streams to identify the state of logistics, traffic, or politics, locally but also globally. However, this data is not sufficient to prepare the company's sales team for their customer pitches.

In this Bachelor's thesis, you will design a two-part dashboard: 
(1) The backend service will collect, store, aggregate, and process information from many different sources.
(2) The frontend — a dashboard — should show this merged information and compile a summary of which actions are immediate and could probably impact the company's core business, either positively or negatively.

The data sources should be manifold and could include macroeconomic data (inflation, employment rate, growth rate), traffic data (cost of gas/diesel, tolls), logistical data (shipping costs), market data for logistics companies (revenue trends), future trends in law and politics regarding logistics/traffic/customs, and current developments regarding sustainability.


This thesis could be done in English or German. For a more detailed (German) description, see the attached PDF.
requirements: There are no hard requirements. However, you should already understand this two-step system, which involves a backend service and a front-facing service. Knowledge of APIs and scraping is beneficial.

title: Interkantonale Disposition von Rettungsdiensten und die Geltung kantonaler Mindeststandards
author: Franziska Sprecher
description:
Interkantonale Disposition von Rettungsdiensten im Schweizer Gesundheitswesen: Konflikte und Harmonisierung kantonaler Mindestanforderungen an Erreichbarkeit und Verfügbarkeit.
Forschungsproblem
Im Zuge der zunehmenden Zentralisierung von Einsatzleitsystemen und der Zusammenlegung von Rettungsdienst-Dispositionen über Kantonsgrenzen hinweg stellt sich die Frage nach der normativen Reichweite kantonaler Rettungsdienstvorgaben. Während Organisation und Finanzierung der Rettungsdienste primär kantonal geregelt sind, operieren moderne Dispositionssysteme zunehmend interkantonal.

Unklar ist dabei insbesondere, welches kantonale Recht Anwendung findet, wenn Rettungsmittel kantonsübergreifend disponiert werden und unterschiedliche Anforderungen an Hilfsfristen, Erreichbarkeit oder Mindestverfügbarkeit bestehen.

Zentrale Forschungsfragen
•	Welche rechtlichen Anforderungen stellen die Kantone an Erreichbarkeit, Verfügbarkeit und Einsatzfristen von Rettungsdiensten?
•	Wie wirken sich interkantonale Dispositionsstrukturen auf die Einhaltung dieser Vorgaben aus?
•	Gilt weiterhin das Recht des territorial zuständigen Kantons, oder entsteht faktisch eine Orientierung am strengsten regulierten Kanton?
•	Bestehen rechtliche Regelungslücken im Bereich der interkantonalen Koordination des Rettungsdienstes?

Wissenschaftlicher Beitrag
Die Arbeit würde eine bislang wenig systematisch untersuchte Schnittstelle zwischen Gesundheitsrecht, Föderalismus und Notfallversorgung beleuchten und mögliche Ansätze für eine interkantonale Harmonisierung oder bundesrechtliche Präzisierung diskutieren.

Die Arbeit steht im Kontext eines vom Nationalfonds geförderten Projekts.
Das Projekt zielt darauf ab, ein datenbasiertes Decision Support System (DSS) zu entwickeln, das mittels Simulation und Optimierung die Planung und Steuerung der präklinischen Versorgung (insb. Rettungsdienste und Notfallversorgung) verbessert und gleichzeitig Qualität und Kosten in Einklang bringt.

Beteiligt sind primär die OST – Ostschweizer Fachhochschule und das Inselspital Bern (Universitätsspital) sowie ein breites Konsortium von über 20 Akteuren aus Rettungsdiensten (EMS), Notfallstationen (ED), Gesundheitsbehörden (HA) und Organisationen wie dem IVR und der Rega.

title: Keeping It Real? The Role of Authenticity in In-Game Advertising for Avatar Identity and Purchase Intentions/Decisions
author: Florian Gasser
description:
Potential Research Question:
How does the perceived authenticity of brand placements in virtual worlds influence avatar identification and purchase intention/decisions?

Description:
In virtual worlds, authenticity plays a crucial role: if an ad feels “forced,” gamers tend to reject it. But when a placement is organically integrated into the game environment, it can strengthen feelings of identity and self-representation through avatars. For brands, this offers a lever to increase purchase intentions via credible integration.

The project could also be written in german - it depends also a little bit on the final research design and sample.
_raw_planned_method: Experiments or Surveys

title: KI (ChatGPT etc.) als Assistenzsystem für Richter und Gerichtsschreiber: rechtsethische und praktische Überlegungen
author: Lukas Müller
description:
Die rasante Entwicklung generativer Künstlicher Intelligenz – insbesondere Sprachmodelle wie ChatGPT – eröffnet auch im Justizwesen neue Anwendungsmöglichkeiten. Die Arbeit untersucht, inwiefern solche Systeme Richter und Gerichtsschreiber bei der juristischen Recherche, Entscheidbegründung oder Texterstellung unterstützen könnten. Im Zentrum stehen rechtsethische und rechtliche Fragen: Welche Anforderungen ergeben sich aus dem Prinzip des fairen Verfahrens, dem Justizgeheimnis oder der richterlichen Unabhängigkeit? Welche Risiken bestehen hinsichtlich Bias, Intransparenz („Black Box“) oder Haftung? Ergänzend sollen praktische Einsatzszenarien analysiert werden – idealerweise durch Interviews mit Justizpraktikern oder exemplarische Tests.

Optional: Studierende mit Interesse an interdisziplinären Methoden können ein einfaches Anwendungsszenario mit ChatGPT, Grok, Deepseek etc. durchspielen und die juristische Qualität der Resultate kritisch evaluieren.

title: Leading Through Contradictions – An Experimental Study on Paradoxical Leadership
author: Nils Fürstenberg
description:
Paradoxical leader behavior refers to a leader’s capacity to integrate seemingly contradictory behaviors—such as maintaining control while allowing autonomy, enforcing standards while being flexible, or balancing closeness and distance toward employees. This approach has been linked to enhanced employee well-being and performance. However, most existing studies rely on correlational data, leaving causal mechanisms largely unexplored.

The goal of this thesis is to design and conduct an online vignette experiment that manipulates paradoxical leader behavior to examine its causal effects on employee well-being and performance, as well as the underlying psychological mechanisms that explain these effects.
requirements: Students should have a solid understanding of or strong interest in experimental methods and quantitative data analysis in R. Prior experience with regression modeling and a willingness to engage with instrumental variable approaches (2SLS) are advantageous.
_raw_planned_method: The project involves (1) developing realistic vignette materials based on prior paradoxical leadership research, (2) implementing the study via an online survey platform (e.g., Qualtrics), and (3) conducting quantitative analyses using regression and two-stage least squares (2SLS) estimation to test direct and indirect effects.

title: Learning to Live with Contradictions: A Qualitative Study on the Antecedents of Paradox Mindsets
author: Nils Fürstenberg
description:
A paradox mindset refers to an individual’s capacity to accept, value, and work productively with tensions and contradictions (e.g., stability vs. change, self vs. others, exploration vs. exploitation). Prior research has shown that a paradox mindset enables leaders and employees to respond more adaptively to complex and competing organizational demands. However, the antecedents of paradox mindsets—that is, the personal experiences, contextual conditions, or socialization processes that foster such thinking—remain largely unexplored.

The goal of this thesis is to identify and understand the antecedents of paradox mindsets through a qualitative study. Possible sources of insight include personal career experiences, formative leadership situations, or organizational practices that helped individuals learn to embrace paradoxes rather than avoid them.
requirements: Students should have a solid understanding of or strong interest in qualitative research methods, particularly interview design, coding, and qualitative content analysis. Familiarity with the critical incident technique is an advantage. The thesis requires good interpretive skills, methodological rigor, and a reflective approach to theory-building.
_raw_planned_method: The project involves conducting semi-structured interviews (e.g., with leaders or professionals) and analyzing them using qualitative content analysis or the critical incident technique to uncover patterns in experiences and mechanisms leading to a paradox mindset.

title: LLM-enabled Workflow Automation of Strategic Management Processes
author: Roger Moser
description:
Build an LLM-enabled workflow that runs in the real world
1. HSG Bachelor and Master theses can be a great fit for a build-and-evaluate project: you design a repeatable workflow for a strategic or international management process, implement it as working automation, and evaluate the impact with a real user group or case company.

2. What you build: You create a production-style workflow using n8n as the automation backbone (its workflows are assembled from “nodes”, including AI nodes such as AI Agent or Basic LLM Chain, and it connects to hundreds of apps and services). You then package and deliver it through Wippli, positioned as a “smart workflow” platform that helps clients and suppliers collaborate end-to-end, with AI-powered features and plugins.

3. What you study (strategic and international management angle) - Pick one high-value process that is typically slow, messy, and decision-heavy, for example:
-- Market-entry intelligence and country risk scanning (policy, competition, partners)
-- Cross-border partner onboarding (due diligence, risk flags, documentation)
-- International negotiation preparation (issue mapping, BATNA options, concession plan)
-- Multi-country stakeholder mapping and engagement planning
-- Global sourcing: supplier comparison, compliance checks, and escalation logic

4. Your research contribution is the decision process redesign: how an LLM-enabled workflow changes cycle time, quality, transparency, and governance (auditability, escalation rules, and human override).
requirements: Strong interest in workflow automation processes and/or developing the required skills, willingness to work on the automation (with humans in the loop) of a strategy process (analysis, development, execution) and publish the results in a HSG controlled marketplace as a reference for your CV.
_raw_planned_method: A combination of conceptual development, workflow automation based on n8n.ai / wippli.com and visualization to create a real-world artefact that the student can showcase to future employers and integrate into the workflow automation showcase (market)place of IIDM-HSG (Prof. Dr. Roger Moser).
