title: Macroeconomics with a Short Memory: Dynamics under Changing Beliefs
author: Johannes Binswanger
description:
In this thesis, you will solve or estimate dynamic macroeconomic models — either simple or more advanced, depending on your preferences — using the Dynare framework. Unlike in standard models, you will explore economies where agents such as households, firms, and policymakers do not fully understand the true structure of the economy. Instead, their decisions are influenced by subjective beliefs or economic narratives (e.g., "inflation is driven by greedy firms" or "tariffs boost domestic industry").

These beliefs can shift over time, leading to time-inconsistent expectations that affect macroeconomic dynamics. Your task will be to investigate how such shifting beliefs can be modeled, simulated, and estimated within Dynare. This will likely involve modifying standard rational expectation frameworks and incorporating behavioral or learning dynamics.

This topic is ideal for students interested in realistic macroeconomic modeling, and who have strong quantitative skills.
requirements: Strong quantitative skills; interest in macroeconomics;
_raw_planned_method: You work with the Dynare framework which is standard in quantitative macroeconomics. You also should have some familiarity with dynamic stochastic general equilibrium (DSGE) models in macroeconomics.
_raw_contact_information: For more information, please send an email to johannes.binswanger@unisg.ch

title: Magical Gaze-based Device Control
author: Simon Mayer
description:
How we look at objects - doors, watches, lamps, etc. - gives cues about our intentions with respect to these objects: "Should I close the door?" - "Do you have to leave?" - "Is it too bright?", etc. Such cues have been documented for several decades (e.g., [1,2]) and the idea to utilize them to inform interactions in smart environments is starting to gain traction in human-robot interaction (e.g., [3]). In tandem, we are witnessing a proliferation of functional device API descriptions.

In this project, you should exploit this dualism: We can find out more and more about what a human intends to do with an object from their gaze, while at the same time we know more and more about what an object can do from its functional description. The objective of this project is to bring these together, enabling gaze-based telekinesis with functionally described smart objects. Conceptually, this will require deriving a theory about how human gaze informs intention with respect to object categories, and to test whether this theory can be generalized. With respect to implementation, it involves creating a classifier that realizes this theory and the creation of a prototype system. Finally, the created system should be validated in a user study: did you actually realize gaze-based telekinesis in an ecologically valid way?
requirements:
Interest in Mixed Reality and Gaze-based Systems
Interest in the Interaction of Ubiquitous Computing Devices and Humans
_raw_planned_method: Our research group is investigating interactions among systems and between systems and people in ubiquitous computing environments. Over the past years, we have developed a keen interest in gaze-based interaction and have published several papers in this domain - from gaze-based activity recognition [4] to the usage of gaze for improving human-agent collaboration [5] and the usage of gaze tracking to evaluate user experiences [6] - are you creeped out about the privacy implications of continuous gaze tracking? We as well, but we have a solution (see [7]) We furthermore supply you with a robust visual object recognition framework [8] to permit your prototype to identify objects and with a laboratory full of devices (mobile and stationary robots as well as different types of sensors) that come with functional API descriptions. This is so that you may concentrate on the core of the gaze-based telekinesis problem from the start.

title: Management with costly information
author: Michèle Müller-Itten
description:
Consider on a choice model where a manager has to assign workers to tasks. Workers differ in their skill abilities, and tasks differ in difficulty. In the simplest case, the manager can hire workers, each may be skilled or unskilled, and assign them to one of two tasks. Only one of the tasks is critical, and only skilled workers can solve it. 
However, it is costly to employ people and it is costly to acquire information about the workers or the tasks. Using the framework of Rational Inattention and building upon an existing model that I have developed, you will analyze how different factors shape the manager's optimal choice: Will she focus on learning about worker skill, so she can avoid unnecessary hires? Will she focus on learning about the tasks, so she can avoid assigning workers to the non-critical task? Or will she do both, even if it is costly? 
You will expand the model to study the impact of learning frictions and risk aversion in this setting (of course you are welcome to generalize it in any other direction too). The goal of the thesis is to link the insights from the model to existing literature from the marketing literature to highlight parallels and generate new insights.
requirements: Ability to do numerical simulations in matlab (or similar), familiarity with the management literature and expected utility models
_raw_planned_method: Theory, numerical simulation and calibration, literature review

title: Master's thesis in the context of medical nutrition in entrepreneurship

You will identify and define potential market opportunities, formulate hypotheses, and validate them through interviews with relevant stakeholders as well as public data sources.
Beyond the hospital setting, the thesis also explores and prioritizes opportunities in other sectors such as long-term care, catering and food services, corporate canteens, sports and consumer health apps, and food waste analytics.
requirements: A concise presentation with findings, competitor benchmarking, and a prioritized opportunity roadmap (incl. app/data product concepts), culminating in clear recommendations for scaling and commercialization. Focus on the international potential.

· Define the hypotheses that must be tested in order to estimate the market opportunity.

title: Proof-of-Presence Framework for Sybil Attack Prevention in Decentralized LoRa/Mesh Networks
author: Karim Khamaisi
description:
This thesis tackles a critical, real-world security challenge in decentralized, off-grid mesh networks like those used for disaster relief (e.g., LoRa/Meshtastic). These networks are highly vulnerable to the Sybil Attack [1], where a single attacker uses one radio to spoof dozens of unique digital identities, overwhelming the network and spreading misinformation [2]. 

Your goal is to design and validate a novel Proof-of-Presence (PoP) framework. This framework will incorporate a security mechanism that allows the network to verify a node's unique physical presence using lightweight cryptographic and radio techniques. This is essential for ensuring that every digital identity represents one true user [3]. 

Example 1 – Defeating the Spoof: A Sybil attacker, using one laptop and one LoRa radio, tries to introduce 50 fake identities. Your PoP framework will detect that all 50 identities share the same physical radio signature (signal strength, timing, etc.) [4]. The framework will then automatically isolate these fraudulent accounts, ensuring the network remains secure and reliable. 

Example 2 – Trustless Identity: Since there's no central server, the PoP framework establishes trust autonomously. It combines simple radio signal measurements with secure verification logic to prove a node's unique location and identity, establishing trust through physics, not a central database [5]. 

Research Questions 

1) How can we combine simple radio metrics with lightweight cryptographic methods to generate a low-overhead Proof-of-Presence (PoP) signature? 

2) Can a PoP framework effectively prevent a Sybil attack with minimal impact on the latency and battery life of resource-constrained LoRa/Mesh devices? 


References 

1. Newsome, J., Shi, E., Song, D., & Perrig, A. (2004, April). The sybil attack in sensor networks: analysis & defenses. In Proceedings of the 3rd international symposium on Information processing in sensor networks (pp. 259-268). 

2. Patel, S. T., & Mistry, N. H. (2017, February). A review: Sybil attack detection techniques in WSN. In 2017 4th International conference on electronics and communication systems (ICECS) (pp. 184-188). IEEE. 

3. Khatri, N., Lee, S., & Nam, S. Y. (2024). Sybil Attack-Resistant Blockchain-Based Proof-of-Location Mechanism with Privacy Protection in VANET. Sensors, 24(24), 8140. 

4. Newsome, J., Shi, E., Song, D., & Perrig, A. (2004, April). The sybil attack in sensor networks: analysis & defenses. In Proceedings of the 3rd international symposium on Information processing in sensor networks (pp. 259-268). 

5. Ruotsalainen, H., Shen, G., Zhang, J., & Fujdiak, R. (2022). LoRaWAN physical layer-based attacks and countermeasures, a review. Sensors, 22(9), 3127.
requirements: Passion for cybersecurity, decentralization, and network protocols. Python skills are essential, with experience in network simulation tools (like NS-3 or Mininet). Familiarity with basic cryptography concepts and an understanding of wireless communication (e.g., LoRa/Wi-Fi) are highly beneficial.
_raw_planned_method: This thesis will be implementation-focused and analytically validated. You will first design the core PoP mechanism and define its verification steps. Next, you will implement this mechanism within a network simulation environment that models a decentralized mesh network, focusing on LoRa/Wi-Fi device characteristics. You will test the framework's effectiveness by simulating a massive Sybil attack, measuring the reduction in fake nodes and the resulting improvement in network health (e.g., message delivery ratio). The outcome will be a validated PoP framework design with a full performance analysis, providing the foundational security layer for future resilient communication systems.

title: Proximity Matching and Collision Avoidance
author: Katerina Mitrokotsa
description: Privacy-preserving fuzzy matching is crucial for applications like biometric identification, GPS-based proximity detection, and illegal content detection, where data is inherently noisy. Existing methods are inefficient when handling high-dimensional or complex data. This thesis will focus on understanding and improving fuzzy private set intersection (FPSI), a technique that allows secure matching of similar data without revealing sensitive information. The goal is to analyze existing FPSI techniques, identify their limitations, and evaluate their potential for improvement while extending their functionalities.
requirements: Cybersecurity/Cryptography course/backbround (MCS, BCS)

2. Implementation and benchmarking,
3. Propose optimizations and extend to other functionalities

title: Responsible Influencing: Communication Strategies for Sustainable Tourism
author: Florian Gasser
description:
Influencer marketing is now a key tool in tourism communication – and at the same time a driver of partial overtourism (e.g., Lake Brienz in Switzerland; Lake Braies in South Tyrol/Italy, etc.). However, more and more destinations are trying to use influencers in a targeted manner for sustainable communication strategies in order to steer visitor flows and focus attention on lesser-known places. This master's thesis examines how such “responsible influencer campaigns” need to be designed in order to contribute to a more conscious and balanced tourism demand.

The aim is to understand which communication factors – such as authenticity, sustainability relevance, or type of presentation – influence the perception and behavior of potential tourists. In doing so, it is possible to examine how different forms of influencer posts affect attitudes, sympathy, and travel intentions. The thesis thus contributes to the question of whether and how influencer marketing can be used as a strategic tool against overtourism.

Keywords: Influencer Marketing / Social Media / Consumer Behaviour / Experimental Designs / Sustainability / Sustainability Communication
_raw_planned_method:
Possible research method:
- Experimental study in which participants evaluate various influencer posts (e.g., on hotspots vs. lesser-known destinations); alternatively, this can be combined with a content analysis of real campaigns.
- Online survey of recipients on their perception of sustainable communication messages.

title: Retail Giants in U.S. Healthcare: Strategic Moves, Outcomes, and Future Di-rections of CVS, Walmart, and Amazon
author: Odile-Florence Giger
description:
Background
Over the past decade, major U.S. retail and technology companies have expanded aggressively into the domestic healthcare sector. CVS Health, Walmart, and Amazon have pursued strategies ranging from pharmacy operations and primary care clinics to telehealth services, health insurance integration, and supply chain optimization. These moves aim to deliver more consumer-oriented, lower-cost, and tech-nology-enabled care to the U.S. population.
While some initiatives have scaled successfully, others have been discontinued or restructured. Analyz-ing these trajectories offers valuable insights into the dynamics of U.S. healthcare disruption, cross-industry convergence, and retail-driven care delivery.

Research Question
How have two major U.S. retail or technology companies (selected from CVS Health, Walmart, and Amazon) entered, evolved, and positioned themselves in the U.S. healthcare market — what has worked, what has not, and why?
Sub-Questions
1.	What have been the key healthcare initiatives of the selected companies to date?
2.	Which of these initiatives have been successful, and which have failed?
3.	What market, operational, regulatory, or cultural factors explain these outcomes?
4.	How do the companies’ future healthcare strategies compare, and what are the potential impli-cations for the U.S. healthcare system?
requirements:
Timeline:
Submission of Thesis in February 2026
_raw_planned_method:
•	Desk Research: Review of academic literature, U.S. industry reports, press releases, investor calls, and case studies.
•	Comparative Case Study: Structured analysis of two selected companies’ healthcare strategies in the U.S. context.
•	Expert Interviews: Semi-structured interviews with U.S. healthcare industry experts, policy spe-cialists, and executives in retail health to validate findings and gain insider perspectives.

title: Rethinking AI Evaluation
author: Christina Niklaus
description:
Accuracy assumes convergence. But what if rational agents disagree?

This project investigates alternative evaluation paradigms for AI systems operating in pluralistic domains. Instead of measuring correctness, we explore coherence, conditional validity, and divergence mapping.

title: Scalable AI-powered Automation of Customer Communication

description:

The final solution should be scalable to hundreds to thousands of emails per day with minimal response time. The focus will increase in difficulty:
(1) Respond to basic requests ("Where is my shipment?")
(2) Respond to intermediate requests ("I need a proof of delivery/an invoice")
(3) Respond to more sophisticated queries: "How much does a shipment from A to B cost?"

In a multi-step evaluation, readily available LLMs and Chatbot frameworks are to be implemented and evaluated.

This thesis will be supported by GEBRÜDER WEISS (https://www.gw-world.com), a global shipment provider.
requirements: While there are no hard requirements, you already bring knowledge of applying LLMs to a problem and know how to develop such a system.

title: Scrutinizing Systemic Risks in Personalized Recommender Systems
author: Jannis Strecker-Bischoff
description:
Social media platforms have become the new public squares, wielding massive influence over how billions of people consume information, form opinions, and take decisions. Yet, beneath the surface of these seemingly personalized experiences lies a profound challenge: the algorithms powering these platforms don’t just recommend content—they shape worldviews, amplify divisions, and steer societal discourse. 

This thesis examines the systemic risks associated with personalized recommender systems on Very Large Online Platforms (VLOPs). While these systems are designed to enhance user engagement through content personalization, they often lead to unintended consequences, such as the formation of filter bubbles, distortion of public opinion, and increased societal polarization. Despite new regulations in the EU, such as the Digital Services Act (DSA), which promote data transparency and accountability from VLOPs, researchers continue to face significant barriers in accessing platform data from platforms like X (formerly Twitter), Instagram, TikTok, and others. 

By employing black-box testing as a methodological framework, this project focuses on identifying, analyzing, assessing, and mitigating systemic risks. Additionally, it evaluates the effectiveness of the DSA in enabling meaningful research on these issues. Given the pivotal role of social media in shaping public discourse—especially during the major global elections of 2024 and the enforcement of the DSA—this research is both timely and critical.
requirements:
This project is great for you, if you are:
- Passionate about or familiar with data scraping tools and working with social media data. 
- Interested in social media platforms and their recommender systems, and curious about how they shape information consumption. 
- Enthusiastic about regulations like the Digital Services Act (DSA) and their potential on data access. 
- Drawn to exploring events and trends influencing the future of social media platforms. 
- Looking to publish your work in academic venues and gain experience in impactful research dissemination. 

Unsure about the topic? See here for additional readings:
DSA: https://reclaimingautonomyonline.notion.site/Researcher-access-to-platform-data-under-the-DSA-Questions-and-answers-8f7390f3ae6b4aa7ad53d53158ed257c#3feb9a106d0c4e77bf0b7d7bc156ddbf Systemic Risks: https://algorithmwatch.org/en/researching-systemic-risks-under-the-digital-services-act/  
Scrutinizing Social Media Platforms: https://www.wsj.com/tech/tiktok-israel-gaza-hamas-war-a5dfa0ee

title: Skins, Status & Social Influence: Wenn Werbung im Game zum Gruppenerlebnis wird
author: Florian Gasser
description:
Virtuelle Welten – etwa in Online-Games, Social-VR-Umgebungen oder Metaverse-Plattformen – sind längst nicht mehr rein spielerische Räume, sondern komplexe soziale Ökosysteme. Nutzer:innen agieren dort in Netzwerken, tauschen sich in Echtzeit aus und gestalten gemeinsam ihre digitale Identität. Kaufentscheidungen – etwa der Erwerb von Skins, virtuellen Items oder Markenprodukten – erfolgen in diesem Kontext häufig nicht isoliert, sondern im Zusammenspiel mit sozialem Druck, Gruppenzugehörigkeit und der Wahrnehmung anderer.

Werbung in diesen Umgebungen (In-Game Advertising) ist daher nicht nur ein individueller Reiz, sondern ein sozial eingebettetes Phänomen. Sie kann Status, Anerkennung oder Zugehörigkeit signalisieren und wird durch soziale Interaktionen, Empfehlungen und Vergleichsprozesse verstärkt oder abgeschwächt. Der soziale Kontext – etwa die Reaktion von Peers, der Austausch in Gaming-Communities oder Beobachtung durch andere Avatare – könnte somit einen entscheidenden Einfluss auf die Werbewirkung und die anschließende Kaufentscheidung haben.

Diese Masterarbeit soll untersuchen, wie soziale Dynamiken in virtuellen Umgebungen die Wahrnehmung und Effektivität von In-Game Advertising prägen. Dabei kann sowohl das Erleben von Werbung als soziales Ereignis als auch die Rolle von Status, Gruppennormen und Identität im digitalen Raum betrachtet werden.
_raw_planned_method: Für dieses Projekt bietet sich ein qualitatives Forschungsdesign an, beispielsweise in Form von leitfadengestützten Interviews oder Fokusgruppen mit Gamer:innen bzw. Jugendlichen, die regelmäßig in virtuellen Welten aktiv sind. Ergänzend könnten Beobachtungen oder netnographische Ansätze (Analyse von Foren, In-Game-Kommunikation oder Streaming-Communities) eingesetzt werden, um ein tieferes Verständnis der sozialen Mechanismen hinter digitalen Kaufentscheidungen zu gewinnen.

title: Smarter Signifier Recommendation for Web-based Agents
author: Jérémy Lemee
description:
Enabling users to interact efficiently and intuitively with devices is one of the core challenges that User Centered Design attempts to solve.
 Designers need to make users aware of the functionalities that the device offers and how they can be used by the users to achieve their own goals. They can do so by making the device expose signifiers that indicate how it can be used [1]. Such signifiers need to be tailored to the intended user. For example, a user able to read only English should not be shown a signifier in another language. While signifiers have initially been developed for humans, new research suggests their uses by software agents. Our proposed mechanism, called the Signifier Exposure Mechanism (SEM) [2,3], enables agents to perceive contextually relevant information, in a Hypermedia Multi-Agent System [4], to help them achieve their own objectives. The SEM matches the signifiers provided by the artifacts found in the environment with the information provided by the profile of the agent in order to determine which signifiers to show the agent so that such signifiers are relevant to the agent. The SEM also enables agents to add their own signifiers for use by other agents and can therefore enable collaboration among agents through cognitive stigmergy [5], a type of stigmergic interaction that is meant to enable cooperation among rational agents. The SEM can be considered a special type of a recommender system to the extent that it recommends signifiers to agents. Therefore machine learning techniques, that can be applied to recommender systems in order to provide ever improving recommendations, can also be applied to enable the SEM to improve itself at run time. Given that SEMs are intended to be used in highly dynamic environments, the use of machine learning is especially crucial to ensure that its recommendations remain appropriate to the agents even if the relevance of the initial recommendation mechanism decreases over time. Machine Learning techniques that are considered include Reinforcement Learning that could be able to improve the SEM based on feedback and Large Language Models to process the (often textual) information provided by signifiers.

[1] Don Norman. 2013. The Design of Everyday Things: Revised and Expanded Edition. Basic Books
[2] Signifiers as a First-class Abstraction in Hypermedia Multi-Agent Systems: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.06970 
[3] Signifiers for conveying and exploiting affordances: from human-computer interaction to multi-agent systems: https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1007/s10472-024-09938-6.pdf 
[4] Ciortea, A., Boissier, O., Ricci, A.: Engineering World-Wide Multi-Agent Systems with Hypermedia. In: International Workshop on Engineering Multi-Agent Systems. Springer, pp. 285–301 (2018) https://www.alexandria.unisg.ch/server/api/core/bitstreams/37e4cdf0-11b2-4ea6-90c4-e21f1e5bb8bf/content
[5] Cognitive Stigmergy: https://lia.disi.unibo.it/~ao/pubs/pdf/2007/e4masIII-rovgo.pdf
requirements:
Interest and Knowledge on Agents/Multi-Agent Systems
Basic Knowledge on Machine Learning (e.g., Reinforcement Learning, Large Language Models).
_raw_planned_method:
The thesis will follow the steps: 
1. Find out how to estimate the relevance of a signifier to an agent. 
2. Enable the recommendation system to learn from agent feedback in order to make better recommendations. 
3. Enable the agent to learn how to generate new contextually relevant signifiers for the agent at run time.

title: Stock Exchanges and the Prediction Market: Strategic Entry, Market Design, and the Transatlantic Regulatory Divide
author: Andreas Grünbichler
description:
Prediction markets—exchange-traded platforms where participants buy and sell binary contracts on the outcomes of future events—have moved from academic curiosity to financial mainstream in under two years. Combined trading volumes on the two largest platforms, Polymarket and Kalshi, exceeded $37 billion in 2025, representing roughly 130-fold growth from early 2024 (International Banker, 2026). The Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), parent company of the New York Stock Exchange, signalled the sector’s institutional legitimacy with a $2 billion strategic investment in Polymarket in October 2025, while CME Group partnered with FanDuel to launch regulated event contracts (U.S. News, 2026). Meanwhile, retail brokerages such as Robinhood have made prediction markets their fastest-growing revenue line, reporting more than 12 billion contracts traded in 2025 (American Banker, 2026).
Yet this explosive growth is almost exclusively a US phenomenon. In Europe, prediction markets remain legally contentious. France’s gaming authority blocked Polymarket in late 2024 for operating as unlicensed gambling, and Belgium, Poland, Italy, and the Netherlands followed with similar enforcement actions in 2025 (Euronews, 2026; EE Gaming, 2026). No EU-level financial regulator has endorsed the sector; Brussels has not issued formal guidance, and the prevailing legal principle—that anything not explicitly permitted remains prohibited—leaves prediction market operators without a viable path to compliance (SBC News, 2026). The regulatory contrast with the United States, where the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) has embraced prediction markets as a form of regulated event derivatives, could not be starker.
For European stock exchanges this asymmetry presents both strategic risk and opportunity. If prediction markets constitute a new asset class with proven demand, European exchanges risk ceding first-mover advantage to US competitors. If, conversely, the sector’s regulatory ambiguity turns out to be intractable in Europe, premature entry could expose exchanges to reputational and compliance costs. 
Should European stock exchanges enter the prediction market space and under which regulatory conditions?
requirements: Market Microstructure and Regulation understanding
_raw_planned_method: Adopt a mixed-methods, comparative case-study design. This approach is appropriate given the research questions’ combination of legal-institutional analysis (which requires qualitative depth) and strategic evaluation (which benefits from structured comparison). The unit of analysis is the regulatory-strategic nexus: the interaction between regulatory frameworks and the strategic options available to exchange operators in each jurisdiction.

Dazu erarbeiten Sie zwei Umsetzungsvarianten und bewerten die damit verbundenen Chancen und Risiken. Zentral sind die Fragen, welche Methoden der Strategic Foresight (z.B. Horizon Scanning, Szenariomethode, Weak Signals) am besten geeignet sind und wie die Entwicklung der Zukünfte bzw. Szenarien aussehen könnte. Wir legen besonderen Wert auf hohe methodische Qualität.

title: Strategic Pivots - Influences on Decision-Making
author: Janine Crivelli
description:
This master thesis project is part of a larger research initiative examining how organizations navigate strategic pivots in response to disruption. This thesis will focus on deepening our understanding of decision-making and collaboration patterns during pivoting processes through a simulation-based experiment. You will set up the predefined experiment in Qualtrics after testing it, starting the data collection, and aim to gather at least 300 responses from entrepreneurs (only complete responses will be counted). After that, you will analyze the data and together, we will discuss the results.

If you're interested, we’d be happy to provide you with more detailed information about the experiment and your potential master thesis.
requirements: We are looking for motivated Master's students with a strong interest in strategic decision-making processes within companies, particularly in startups or innovation-driven organizations. Initiative, analytical thinking, and enthusiasm for interdisciplinary research and entrepreneurial topics are essential.
_raw_planned_method:
Scenario-based methods delivered via online survey platform (Qualtrics).
This master thesis will employ a between-subjects factorial design to investigate the impact of variables on pivot likelihood.
Participants will be randomly assigned (using a random number generator in the survey software; could consider blocking by gender or another factor of interest) to one of eight experimental conditions in a 2 (PPSR: high/low) x 2 (DC: supportive/opposing) x 2 (RS: internal/external attribution) design. The experiment will be conducted using online survey methods, presenting participants with written scenarios.

Analyses will use linear regression models to test the effects of variables framing on the primary DV pivot likelihood (0-100 scale) and the secondary DV pivot decision (yes/no).

author: Katerina Mitrokotsa

title: Tool for Text Annotation with Eye tracking Support
author: Rositsa Ivanova
description:
To teach an algorithm to grade a task, one needs to know what the correct answer is. While this may seem trivial, humans oftentimes cannot agree on the "gold standard" answer that easily. The task becomes even more complex when one has to assess argumentative texts - the guidelines become more complex, the humans (i.e. annotators) get tired after a while, etc.
By understanding what factors make the task challenging and contribute to the differences in the scoring of annotators, we aim to create an improved assessment tool, which can use the input of Eye tracking devices to aid the annotators, monitor their cognitive load, and detect potential biases in their answers.  

In this project you should focus on the interaction of annotators with an annotation tool. On the one side, we want to ensure that the annotation tool fits the task it is meant to be used for. On the other side, we want to ensure that we address the difficulties that annotators face while completing the task. The objective of this project is to balance out the requirements of the task with the needs of the annotators.
requirements:
Interest in user-friendly interfaces and human-computer interaction.
General programming skills or willingness to improve them.

title: Transparency in Personalized Mixed Reality Interfaces
author: Jannis Strecker-Bischoff
description:
Personalization describes the adaptation of a system based on personal data (e.g, demographics, level of expertise, real-time physiological data, user context data). The personalization of an interface in Mixed Reality (MR) can be very beneficial for users since it, e.g., enables them to interact more efficiently or use the interface based on their abilities and capabilities. For instance, to control a robotic arm in a human-robot collaboration scenario, an expert user could see a more complex interface with more fine-grained control options (see the right figure) than a novice user (see the left figure). Additionally, the interface might be adapted based on their preferred language, current cognitive state, or the task they are currently performing. However, users might not know why the interface is adapted, if they are not informed about it in a well-designed manner. Especially when many parameters are part of the personalization, it can be challenging to design an appropriate interface that does not lead to an increased cognitive load for users.

In this project, you will study how users can be informed transparently about why and how an interface has been personalized for them, to increase their trust in the system, and you will implement a system that makes personalization processes transparent for users. Have you ever clicked the "Why do I see this Ad" button on a (Google-brokered) online advertisement? That's the idea, but we would like to deepen this, and transport it to Mixed Reality systems (see Figures).
requirements:
This project is great for you, if you are:
- Strongly interested or familiar in designing Mixed/Augmented Reality interfaces
- Familiar with visual design tools (e.g., Adobe Photoshop, Figma, Penpot, or Affinity Designer)
- Interested in Personalization, Transparency & Technology
- Interested in participating in state of the art research
- Interested in publishing your research work in academic venues
- An excellent communicator in English
_raw_planned_method:
In this project, you will:
- Review existing approaches for informing users transparently about why and how an (MR) interface has been personalized for them
- Design and create mock-ups of these interface designs (e.g., using Figma or Penpot)
- Conduct and evaluate in person and online surveys to measure people’s preferred design(s) based on the created mock-ups
- Implement the preferred designs (based on the survey results) of transparent personalization communication in an MR application for the Microsoft HoloLens 2 using Unity
- Conduct and evaluate a user study to measure people’s preferred design(s) in one or multiple concrete scenarios

If you are a Master's student, you will additionally:
- Implement options for users to adapt the personalization parameters ("customization").
- Conduct and evaluate a user study to evaluate the transparent design and the adaption of personalization parameters.

title: Trend Estimation and Deseasonalization of the Consumer Spending Index (CSI)
author: Matthias Reginald Fengler
description:
The Consumer Spending Index (CSI) 
https://consumerspendingindex.com/
is a recently developed indicator capturing short-term dynamics in Swiss consumer spending, as observed from card transaction data. While the raw data available on our website provide valuable insights into daily and weekly fluctuations of consumer spending, many users are equally interested in long-term developments and structural trends.

The objective of this thesis is to investigate and compare modern methods for trend estimation and deseasonalization of time series data, with the aim of producing a smoothed, long-term version of the CSI. Possible methods include, but are not limited to, STL (Seasonal-Trend decomposition using Loess), X-13ARIMA-SEATS, TBATS (Trigonometric, Box–Cox, ARMA, Trend, and Seasonal components), Dynamic Harmonic Regression (DHR), Prophet (by Facebook/Meta), and Seasonal State-Space Models / Unobserved Components Models (UCM).

The thesis should assess the practical usefulness and robustness of these methods when applied to high-frequency consumer spending data. The findings are expected to support the development of a publicly available long-term trend series of the CSI and its subseries.
requirements: You must have taken a solid time series class. Interest in programming and data analysis.
_raw_planned_method: Exploration of various trend-seasonality decomposition methods.

title: Vertiefte rechtsvergleichende Analyse ausgewählter kantonaler Rettungsdienstmodelle Regulierung und Organisation des Rettungsdienstes im kantonalen Vergleich
author: Franziska Sprecher
description:
Forschungsproblem:
Während eine breite Analyse Unterschiede zwischen Kantonen sichtbar machen kann, bleibt oft unklar, wie sich diese Unterschiede konkret in der rechtlichen Ausgestaltung und praktischen Organisation des Rettungsdienstes manifestieren.
Eine vertiefte rechtsvergleichende Untersuchung von Kantonen ermöglicht es, die Zusammenhänge zwischen gesetzlicher Regelung, Governance-Strukturen und operativer Umsetzung genauer zu analysieren.
Vorgeschlagen werden die Kantone St. Gallen und Zürich, da hier entsprechende Kontakte bestehen. 

Zentrale Forschungsfragen:
•	Wie unterscheiden sich ausgewählte Kantone in der rechtlichen Struktur ihres Rettungsdienstsystems?
•	Welche Governance-Modelle bestehen (z. B. staatlich organisiert, öffentlich-private Kooperationen, Konzessionsmodelle)?
•	Wie werden zentrale Aspekte wie Qualitätsanforderungen, Planung, Finanzierung und Aufsicht geregelt?
•	Welche rechtlichen Faktoren beeinflussen die Effizienz und Koordination der Rettungsdienste?

Wissenschaftlicher Beitrag:
Die Arbeit würde eine detaillierte juristische Vergleichsstudie von kantonalen Rettungsdienstsystemen liefern und damit vertiefte Einblicke in die Funktionsweise föderaler Gesundheitsregulierung im Bereich der präklinischen Notfallversorgung ermöglichen.

Die Arbeit steht im Kontext eines vom Nationalfonds geförderten Projekts.
Das Projekt zielt darauf ab, ein datenbasiertes Decision Support System (DSS) zu entwickeln, das mittels Simulation und Optimierung die Planung und Steuerung der präklinischen Versorgung (insb. Rettungsdienste und Notfallversorgung) verbessert und gleichzeitig Qualität und Kosten in Einklang bringt.

Beteiligt sind primär die OST – Ostschweizer Fachhochschule und das Inselspital Bern (Universitätsspital) sowie ein breites Konsortium von über 20 Akteuren aus Rettungsdiensten (EMS), Notfallstationen (ED), Gesundheitsbehörden (HA) und Organisationen wie dem IVR und der Rega.

title: Weiterentwicklung der Kostenrechnung als Führungsinstrument im Gesundheitswesen: Fallstudie in einem Schweizer Kantonsspital mit einem Beratungsunternehmen
author: Matthias Mitterlechner
description:
Im Rahmen eines Transformationsprogramms unterzieht sich ein Zentrumsspital in Zusammenarbeit mit einer führenden Beratungsgesellschaft einem umfassenden Prozess-Reengeniering. Dabei werden diverse Prozesse aber auch das Operating Model, also die Organisation angepasst. Im Rahmen der Organisationsentwicklung werden medizinische Plattformen (Ambulant, OP/Anästhesie/Intensivmedizin, Diagnostik/Therapien) neu konzipiert. 

Ziel ist eine klare Abgrenzung zwischen Leistungserbringern von Querschnittsleistungen (Plattformen) und Leistungsbezügern von Querschnittsleistungen (Kliniken). Dies erfordert ein präzises internes Steuerungs- und Verrechnungsmodell. Service Level Agreements (SLAs) definieren Leistungsumfang, Qualitätsstandards, Verfügbarkeit und Schnittstellen. Für eine wirksame Steuerung müssen diese SLA-Komponenten jedoch mit einem dynamischen Kosten- und Leistungsrechnungssystem (KLR) verknüpft werden, welches die heute im Spital verbreiteten tarifbedingten Umlageschlüssel nach REKOLE® für die interne Steuerung ablösen können. 

Die heutige KLR bildet komplexe Leistungsbeziehungen, Vorhalteleistungen und variable Prozesskosten lediglich mit Hilfe von Umlagen auf Vollkostenbasis ab, Mengen- resp. Beschäftigungsabweichungen können nicht gesteuert werden. Daraus fehlt eine einheitliche Grundlage für interne Verrechnungspreise von bezogenen Leistungen, die sowohl Transparenz schaffen als auch gewünschtes steuerndes Verhalten fördern. 

Ein ergänzendes Element kann eine Dynamisierung der Preise sein, die z.B. Verhalten auf prozessual weniger attraktive Zeiten (z.B. OP Freitagnachmittags) lenkt. Es ist die Frage zu prüfen, ob und wie Fix- und Gemeinkosten in die Kalkulationssätze einfliessen müssten (Herstellkostensystematik). 

Die Masterarbeit hat zum Ziel, das KLR-System so weiterzuentwickeln, dass es SLA-Kriterien widerspiegelt, Prozess- und Kapazitätsdaten integriert und eine faire, nachvollziehbare und strategiekonforme Leistungsverrechnung ermöglicht. 

Dieses Thema ist zentral für die zukünftige Governance der medizinischen Plattformen und wirkt sich direkt auf Effizienz, Rollenverständnis und wirtschaftliche Steuerbarkeit der Organisation aus.
requirements:
Zeitraum: Sommer 2026 - Sommer 2027
Freude an der Verknüpfung von Führung, Organisations-/Strategieentwicklung und Kostenrechnung
_raw_planned_method: Konzeptionelle Entwicklung und empirische Simulation mit Echtdaten. Enge Zusammenarbeit mit CFO und Beratungsunternehmen.

title: When Ambiguity Blurs Moral Lines – An Experimental Study on Paradoxical Leadership and Moral Disengagement
author: Nils Fürstenberg
description:
Paradoxical leadership involves integrating seemingly contradictory behaviors, such as combining control with autonomy, enforcing strict standards while allowing flexibility, or balancing closeness and distance toward employees. While this style can help teams navigate complex, competing demands, its inherent ambiguity may also create confusion about what is right or wrong, and what is really expected. Such ambiguity could unintentionally facilitate moral disengagement, that is, psychological mechanisms by which individuals justify, minimize, or reframe unethical behavior.

The goal of this thesis is to experimentally investigate how paradoxical leader behavior shapes moral disengagement and related outcomes (e.g., unethical behavioral intentions, perceived norm clarity). The central focus is on whether paradoxical leadership, compared to more consistent leadership behavior, increases or decreases tendencies to morally disengage in ethically challenging situations.
requirements: Students should have a strong interest in leadership, ethics, and social-cognitive mechanisms, as well as familiarity or strong motivation to learn experimental methods and quantitative data analysis in R. Prior experience with regression analysis and survey-based experiments is advantageous.
_raw_planned_method: The thesis will use an online vignette experiment in which participants are exposed to leadership scenarios that systematically vary in the degree of paradoxical behavior (and potentially in moral norm clarity). Outcome measures will capture moral disengagement and related attitudinal or behavioral intentions. Data will be analyzed using quantitative methods (e.g., regression, moderated/mediated regression).

title: When Teams Think Together – Leadership’s Role in Shaping Shared Mental Models
author: Nils Fürstenberg
description:
Team mental models refer to the shared understandings that team members develop about their tasks, roles, and collaboration processes. They are essential for coordination, adaptability, and team performance. Leadership plays a crucial role in fostering, aligning, and updating these shared mental models, yet research on this relationship remains fragmented across different theoretical and methodological traditions.

The goal of this thesis is to conduct a systematic literature review that synthesizes existing empirical findings on how leadership behaviors influence team mental models. The review should map conceptual frameworks, summarize methodological approaches, and identify key antecedents, mechanisms, and outcomes.
requirements: Students should be familiar with systematic literature review methods, including database searches, inclusion/exclusion criteria, and qualitative or quantitative synthesis. Strong analytical and conceptual skills are required to integrate diverse theoretical perspectives on leadership and team cognition.
_raw_planned_method: The thesis will follow systematic review protocols (e.g., PRISMA) to identify, screen, and code relevant studies. Students will analyze and structure findings to develop an integrative framework and highlight directions for future research.

title: Zukunft der berufsbegleitenden Weiterbildung
author: Samuel Heer
description: Die Nachfrage nach berufsbegleitender Weiterbildung verändert sich durch Digitalisierung und neue Arbeitsformen. Welche Trends prägen die Zukunft der berufsbegleitenden Weiterbildung? Wie verändern digitale Technologien Weiterbildungsangebote von Hochschulen? Welche Erwartungen haben Berufstätige an Weiterbildungsprogramme? Welche Rolle spielen Hochschulen im System des lebenslangen Lernens?
